Outline
Eduarda, I read your deal memo. The content is right, but it reads like an essay.
Understood. I will lead with the headline, the recommendation in the first line.
Good. And the key terms? They are buried in long sentences.
I will move them into bullet points. And I will write the terms agreed last week, not the terms that were agreed.
Exactly. Cut every clause you can. What about the reason for the recommendation?
I will state the rationale in one line, and define the scope so no one assumes more.
Perfect. One page, no more. Send it to me before you share it.
Will do. I will send you the draft first, then circulate the final version to the partners.
Headline: we recommend proceeding with the deal.
The deal closing on Friday is on track.
Use bullet points for the key terms, not paragraphs.
Rationale: the returns exceed our threshold.
I will circulate the draft for review.

Hi Eduarda, one note before you circulate that deal memo. The content is right, but it is too long. Lead with the headline, the recommendation, in the first line, so a busy partner gets it in five seconds. Then use bullet points, not paragraphs, for the key terms. Cut every clause you can. Instead of the terms that were agreed last week, just write the terms agreed last week. State the rationale in one sentence, and keep the scope tight. A memo is not an essay. One page, no more. Send me the draft before it goes out.

Hi team, a quick reminder on how we write a deal memo here. Every memo opens with a headline that states the recommendation up front. The reader should never hunt for the point. Keep it concise: cut the words that add nothing. The deal closing on Friday is shorter and stronger than the deal which is closing on Friday. Use bullet points for terms and numbers. Give the rationale in one line. Define the scope so no one assumes more than we promised. Then circulate the draft for review before it reaches the partners.

Eduarda opened the memo with a headline stating the recommendation. She listed the key terms as bullet points. She gave the rationale in one clear line. She defined the scope of the deal. She circulated the draft to her director for review.
